title: Broken swap invariant allows draining nearly an entire reserve for dust-sized input
claim: `swap()` computes post-swap balances on a 10,000-based scale and subtracts a 0.1% fee via `amountIn * 10`, but it still compares the result against `_reserve0 * _reserve1 * 1000**2` instead of `10000**2`. The invariant check is therefore 100x too weak and accepts massively undercollateralized swaps.
impact: Any attacker can permissionlessly steal almost all of either reserve with only a dust input, directly draining LP funds and rendering the pool price state meaningless.
paths: If reserves are `(R0,R1)`, an attacker can pre-send 1 wei of `token0` and call `swap(0, R1-1, attacker, "")`; `amount0In > 0` and the weakened K-check still passes.
The attack is symmetric for `token0` by pre-paying a dust amount of `token1` and requesting nearly all of `reserve0` as output.

title: Pair token bindings remain mutable if the factory ever re-calls `initialize()`
claim: `initialize()` only checks `msg.sender == factory` and never records that initialization has already happened. A later factory call can overwrite `token0` and `token1` for an already funded pair.
impact: If the factory later gains an unintended arbitrary-call path, is upgraded unsafely, or is otherwise misused, existing pools can be rebound to attacker-chosen token addresses. The pair would then mint, burn, swap, skim, and sync against the wrong assets, potentially orphaning the real reserves and bricking redemptions.
paths: Users deposit liquidity into a legitimate pair.
At a later time, the factory invokes `initialize()` again with different token addresses.
Subsequent pair operations reference the new token contracts, while the original underlying tokens remain stranded in the pair address.
